title: Cat Unilateral Nasal Discharge: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Notice discharge from only one of your cat's nostrils? Learn about potential causes, why it is a medium-urgency issue, and how a photo helps your vet.
content_html:
Understanding Unilateral Nasal Discharge in Cats

Noticing discharge from only one of your cat's nostrils (unilateral) is a specific symptom that differs from common respiratory infections where both nostrils are typically affected. We understand how concerning it is to see your feline friend uncomfortable, but identifying this one-sided issue is a helpful first step in getting them the right care.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for unilateral nasal discharge is Medium. While it is usually not a life-threatening emergency that requires a midnight trip to the ER, it is not a condition that will resolve on its own. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. However, if your cat is also experiencing severe lethargy, facial swelling, or labored breathing, you should seek immediate care.

Possible Causes

When discharge is localized to one side, veterinarians often look for specific triggers. Common causes include a foreign object (like a blade of grass) lodged in the nasal passage, a dental abscess where a tooth root infection has reached the sinus, or nasal polyps. In some cases, it may indicate a localized fungal infection or even a tumor, especially in senior cats.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Because cats are fastidious groomers, they often lick away discharge before you arrive at the clinic. Taking a high-quality photo or video of your cat's nose and face while the discharge is visible provides the vet with vital information. It helps them identify the color (clear, yellow, or bloody) and consistency, and allows them to check for subtle facial asymmetry or swelling that might not be as apparent during the physical exam.

faqs.question: Can I treat my cat's runny nose with over-the-counter medicine?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat human medications. Since one-sided discharge is often caused by a physical obstruction or a dental issue, topical or oral human cold medicines will not help and can be highly toxic to cats.

faqs.question: What if the discharge contains blood?
faqs.answer: If you see blood (epistaxis) coming from one nostril, you should move from a medium-urgency mindset to a high-urgency one. Bloody discharge can indicate trauma, advanced infection, or more serious internal conditions that require prompt evaluation.

faqs.question: How will a veterinarian diagnose the cause?
faqs.answer: A vet will likely start with a physical and dental exam. Depending on the findings, they may recommend X-rays of the skull or teeth, a sedated nasal exam to look for foreign objects, or a rhinoscopy.
